blocks.content: He told himself he would. Walk back. Maybe stop at The Copper Mug. Let the day settle into something he could understand.

Instead he walked slowly, the evening pulling long shadows across the sidewalk, Gerald hanging from his hand — that ridiculous yellow duck with the crooked bow tie, a consolation prize from the claw machine Cassandra had convinced him to try. He'd lost four tokens before winning it. He'd handed it to her immediately. She'd handed it back and said 'he's yours, I can tell,' which made no sense and which he had not argued with.

He slowed without meaning to near the park. The fountain sat in its usual place, still sputtering that thin, uneven stream upward. Yesterday it was just a fountain. Tonight it was where something started.
He stood at the edge of the stone path for a moment. The bench near the archway was empty.

By the time he reached his apartment, the sky had gone purple at the edges. He set Gerald on his desk. Sat on the edge of his bed. Looked at the ceiling. His mind kept doing the thing where it replayed a specific moment — not the whole day, just the one — the way she'd looked at his painting. Not politely. Not in the careful, uncommitted way people looked at things when they were trying to be kind. Actually looked. Like it was saying something she understood.
His phone was in his hand. He didn't remember picking it up.
He read it back. Too small for how it felt. He sent it anyway.
The reply came faster than expected.
Not a question. Nathan didn't use question marks for things that mattered.
Park. She walked up to me while I was painting.
A stranger walked up to you.
And you didn't immediately pack up and leave.
Ethan stared at that. Because that was exactly what he usually did. He had a whole system — polite nod, closed sketchbook, exit. Quick and bloodless.
We talked. Went to the arcade. She works there.
Last year you said they were sensory punishment dressed up with prizes.
Ethan set the phone on the bed. Picked it up again.

How was he supposed to explain it? The way she talked — like every word was chosen but also like she couldn't help herself. The way she'd looked at the fountain and found something to say about it that he'd never thought of in the four years he'd been walking past it. The specific warmth that had come into his chest when she laughed at something he said, the way a heater kicks on in a room that had gotten cold without your noticing.
I don't know how to say it.
You don't have to say it.
That you actually let her in.
Which means you didn't have time to decide not to.
Ethan sat with that. Nathan wasn't wrong. He was never wrong about this. That was the problem.
